questions.q: What was Aryabhata, launched in 1975?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was India's first satellite, marking the country's entry into space.

questions.q: In which year was the Aryabhata satellite launched?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was launched on 19 April 1975.

questions.q: Which Indian space organisation built the Aryabhata satellite?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was designed and built by the Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO).

questions.q: After whom was the Aryabhata satellite named?
questions.explanation: The satellite was named after Aryabhata, the celebrated 5th-century Indian mathematician-astronomer.

questions.q: The launch of Aryabhata marked India's entry into which field?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata's launch marked India's entry into space as a satellite-building nation.

questions.q: Aryabhata was an artificial satellite placed where?
options: On the surface of the Moon
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was an Earth-orbiting artificial satellite.

questions.q: With which country's help was the Aryabhata satellite launched into orbit?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was launched by the Soviet Union using a Soviet rocket.

questions.q: Aryabhata is best described as which type of milestone for India?
options: The first Indian to walk on the Moon
questions.explanation: Aryabhata is celebrated as India's very first satellite.

questions.q: What did Aryabhata help establish for India?
options: Indigenous capability to design and build satellites
questions.explanation: Building Aryabhata gave India indigenous experience in designing and fabricating a satellite.

questions.q: Aryabhata's launch year of 1975 falls in which decade?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was launched in 1975, during the 1970s.

questions.q: On which exact date was Aryabhata launched?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was launched on 19 April 1975.

questions.q: From which country's launch site was Aryabhata sent into orbit?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was launched from the Soviet rocket launch and development site at Kapustin Yar.

questions.q: Approximately how much did the Aryabhata satellite weigh at launch?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata weighed about 360 kilograms at launch.

questions.q: What was the approximate geometric shape of the Aryabhata satellite?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was a 26-sided polyhedron (a near-spherical multi-faceted body).

questions.q: Who was the chairman of ISRO at the time Aryabhata was built and launched?
questions.explanation: Satish Dhawan was ISRO chairman in 1975, having succeeded Vikram Sarabhai after his death in 1971.

questions.q: Which scientist served as the project director who led the team that built Aryabhata?
questions.explanation: U. R. Rao led the team that designed and built Aryabhata, India's first satellite.

questions.q: What kind of scientific experiments was Aryabhata designed to carry?
options: Experiments in X-ray astronomy, aeronomics and solar physics
questions.explanation: Aryabhata carried experiments in X-ray astronomy, aeronomics (upper-atmosphere study) and solar physics.

questions.q: What ended Aryabhata's scientific data collection only a few days after launch?
options: A failure in the electrical power system
questions.explanation: A failure in the satellite's power system cut short its experiments after about four days of operation.

questions.q: The Aryabhata satellite was honoured by featuring on which everyday Indian item?
options: Postage stamps only of foreign nations
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was depicted on the reverse of the Indian two-rupee banknote, commemorating the achievement.

questions.q: Aryabhata's launch made India one of the early nations to build its own satellite during which era?
options: The Cold War / space age of the 1970s
questions.explanation: Aryabhata's 1975 launch placed India among the satellite-building nations during the Cold War space age.

questions.q: U. R. Rao, the man who led the Aryabhata project, later went on to hold which top post?
questions.explanation: U. R. Rao later became Chairman of ISRO, building on his work as Aryabhata's project director.

questions.q: Aryabhata was launched under an agreement that gave India access to a Soviet launch in exchange for what?
options: Use of Indian ports for tracking and related support
questions.explanation: Under the Indo-Soviet cooperation, the USSR provided the launch while India offered tracking and port facilities for Soviet ships.

questions.q: Which Soviet rocket was used to launch Aryabhata?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was launched aboard a Soviet Kosmos-3M rocket from Kapustin Yar.

questions.q: Near which Indian city was Aryabhata largely designed and assembled?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was built by ISRO at facilities near Bengaluru, where the satellite team was based.

questions.q: Roughly how long did Aryabhata successfully transmit scientific signals before its power system failed?
questions.explanation: After about four days of operation, a power-system failure ended the satellite's scientific transmissions.

questions.q: Aryabhata was placed into what kind of orbit around the Earth?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was placed in a relatively low Earth orbit, not the high geostationary orbit used by communication satellites.

questions.q: What powered the Aryabhata satellite's onboard systems?
questions.explanation: Like most satellites of its era, Aryabhata was powered by solar cells that charged onboard batteries.

questions.q: Which of these was NOT one of the scientific disciplines Aryabhata's experiments targeted?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata's payload covered X-ray astronomy, solar physics and aeronomics, not marine biology.

questions.q: Vikram Sarabhai, who founded the Indian space programme, did NOT live to see Aryabhata launch because he died in which year?
questions.explanation: Vikram Sarabhai died in 1971, four years before Aryabhata was launched in 1975.

questions.q: What was Aryabhata's primary purpose, given its short mission?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata was an experimental scientific and technology-demonstration satellite, proving India could build and operate one.
